_comment: Australian private health insurance industry statistics — APRA quarterly data and AIHW reports
_note: Statistics based on most recent available APRA quarterly data (June 2025 quarter) and 2025-26 industry reports.
source: APRA / AIHW / Private Health Insurance Ombudsman

participation._comment: Proportion of Australian population with PHI cover
participation.hospitalCoverDescription: 45.4% of Australians (12.53 million people) hold hospital cover
participation.extrasCoverDescription: 55.1% of Australians (15.3 million people) hold extras cover
participation.trend: Hospital participation has been slowly declining from ~47% in 2015. Extras participation has remained more stable.

goldDowngrades.description: Approximately 400,000 policyholders downgraded from Gold to lower tiers in the 12 months to June 2025
goldDowngrades.driverDescription: Cost-of-living pressures driving downgrades — 4.41% premium increase in 2026 (highest since 2017)

benefitsReturn._comment: How much of each premium dollar is returned as benefits (claims paid / premiums collected)
benefitsReturn.hospitalBenefitsDescription: For every $1 in hospital premiums, approximately 83 cents is paid back in hospital benefits. Down from ~90% pre-pandemic.
benefitsReturn.extrasBenefitsDescription: For every $1 in extras premiums, less than 45 cents is returned in extras benefits. The remainder covers insurer administration, profit margins, and risk equalisation.
benefitsReturn.combinedBenefitsDescription: Combined hospital + extras benefits return ratio is approximately 85 cents per dollar
benefitsReturn.implication: Extras cover is statistically poor value for the average person — most people pay more in premiums than they claim back. Hospital cover provides much better return but is insurance against catastrophic costs, not a savings product.

gapPayments._comment: Out-of-pocket costs patients pay above what Medicare + insurer cover
gapPayments.averageGapGrowthDescription: Average gap increased 9.4% year-on-year to June 2025
gapPayments.noGapRateDescription: 87.1% of in-hospital services were provided with no out-of-pocket gap — meaning the doctor accepted the insurer's payment in full
gapPayments.knownGapDescription: 7.2% of services had a pre-agreed 'known gap' — patient knew the gap amount before treatment
gapPayments.unknownGapDescription: 5.7% of services had an unknown gap — patient may not have known the full cost before treatment
gapPayments.trend: No-gap rates have been declining gradually — down from ~89% five years ago. Average gap amounts are rising faster than inflation.
gapPayments.medianGapDescription: When a gap is charged, the median out-of-pocket amount is approximately $250

waitingPeriods._comment: Standard waiting periods for new PHI policies — set by law

standard.description: 12-month waiting period for pre-existing conditions and most hospital procedures

psychiatric.description: 2-month waiting period for psychiatric care, rehabilitation, and palliative care

obstetric.description: 12-month waiting period for pregnancy and birth-related services

preExisting.description: 12-month waiting period for any condition you had signs/symptoms of in the 6 months before joining

general.description: 2-month waiting period for most extras services

major.description: 12-month waiting period for major dental (crowns, bridges, orthodontics)

premiumIncrease2026.description: Average 4.41% premium increase from 1 April 2026 — highest average increase since 2017 (3.95%)
premiumIncrease2026.context: Driven by rising hospital costs, increased claims post-COVID, and declining participation spreading costs across fewer members

keyMetrics._comment: Summary metrics used across the calculator for context and comparison
keyMetrics.cheapestBasicSingleDescription: Approximate cheapest Basic hospital-only policy for a single adult (before rebate) — enough to avoid MLS
keyMetrics.extrasValueProposition: The average Australian claims less in extras benefits than they pay in extras premiums — extras cover is a convenience product, not a financial saving for most people
